overview.body: Work through each Gaddis program in order. Chapter 4 is where your programs stop being single-pass and start doing real work. Type every program by hand and test each one — loops are where most beginners hit their first infinite loop, and you need to experience that (and fix it) before the Lab assignments.
objectives: Use a sentinel-controlled while loop to repeat until the user says stop
Use a condition-controlled while loop that runs until a value meets a threshold
Use a for loop with a list to iterate through a fixed sequence
Use range() to repeat code a specific number of times
Use range(start, end) and range(start, end, step) for custom sequences
Build an accumulator variable to calculate a running total
Use an inner while loop for input validation
Use nested for loops to process multi-dimensional data
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 4 covers while loops (sentinel-controlled and condition-controlled), for loops, the range() function with one, two, and three arguments, accumulators, input validation loops, and nested loops.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 4: Repetition Structures
programRequirements: Each program is saved as its own individual .py file
Each file uses the exact filename listed in the instructions
Each program runs without syntax errors
Output matches what is shown in the textbook
overview.filenameNote: This chapter uses 10 individual files. Submit all 10 files together in your final CodeGrade submission.

rows.task: for loop with a list literal
Input validation with nested while loops
Nested for loops with per-student accumulator

content.body: A commission calculator that uses a sentinel-controlled while loop. The user enters sales data and gets a commission amount, then decides whether to run the loop again by entering 'y'. The loop stops when anything other than 'y' is entered.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-1.
Create a new file named commission.py.
Type the program exactly as shown, including the keep_going initialization before the loop.
Run it and process at least two commissions, then exit.
Run it again and enter something other than 'y' after the first commission to verify the loop stops.

content.title: Initialize the Sentinel Variable Before the Loop
content.body: The while loop checks keep_going before the first iteration. If keep_going doesn't exist yet, Python raises a NameError. Always initialize your loop control variable before the while statement.

A temperature monitor that loops as long as the input temperature exceeds a MAX_TEMP constant. The user is prompted to adjust the thermostat and re-enter the temperature until it falls within the acceptable range.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-2.
Create a new file named temperature.py.
Type the program including the MAX_TEMP constant.
Run it and enter a temperature above 102.5 — confirm the loop runs.
Enter a lower temperature each time until the loop exits.

content.title: Update the Variable Inside the Loop
content.body: The while loop checks temperature on every iteration. If you never update temperature inside the loop, the condition never changes and the loop runs forever. Press Ctrl+C (Windows) or Command+Period (Mac) to stop an infinite loop.
The simplest possible for loop: iterate through a literal list [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and print each number. Short program, but it establishes the for loop syntax before range() is introduced.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-9 (6th Ed) or Program 4-4 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named simple_loop1.py.
Type the program exactly as shown.
Run it and confirm the numbers 1 through 5 print on separate lines.

content.title: Edition Note
content.body: This program is 4-9 in the 6th Edition and 4-4 in the 5th Edition. The content is the same — only the program number differs. Use whichever edition you have.
A for loop that uses range(5) to print 'Hello world!' exactly five times. This is the standard introduction to range() — the most common way to control how many times a loop runs.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-12 (6th Ed) or Program 4-7 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named simple_loop4.py.
Type the program exactly as shown.
Run it and confirm exactly five lines of output appear.

content.title: range(5) Generates 0 Through 4
content.body: range(5) produces 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — five values starting at zero, not one. The loop variable x gets each of these values, but since we don't use x in the print statement, the output is the same five times.
A table of numbers 1 through 10 and their squares, formatted with tab stops to create aligned columns. Introduces range(1, 11) and the ** exponentiation operator.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-13 (6th Ed) or Program 4-8 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named squares.py.
Type the program including the header lines.
Run it and confirm the table goes from 1 to 10 with correct squares.

content.title: range(1, 11) Not range(1, 10)
content.body: range(start, end) goes from start up to but NOT including end. To get numbers 1 through 10, you need range(1, 11). Using range(1, 10) only gives you 1 through 9.

A KPH-to-MPH conversion table that uses range(START_SPEED, END_SPEED, INCREMENT) to step from 60 to 130 in increments of 10. This is the three-argument form of range(), and the constants make the step size easy to change.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-14 (6th Ed) or Program 4-9 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named speed_converter.py.
Type the program including all four named constants.
Run it and confirm the table starts at 60 KPH and steps by 10 up to 130 KPH.

content.title: Three-Argument range()
content.body: range(start, end, step) lets you count by any amount. range(60, 131, 10) generates 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130. The end value 131 is used instead of 130 because range excludes the end — 130 itself would be missed with 130 as the end.

The squares table from Checkpoint 5, but now the user controls the starting and ending numbers. Demonstrates how to use input values directly in range() and why end + 1 is needed to make the range inclusive.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-16 (6th Ed) or Program 4-11 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named user_squares2.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and enter a start of 3 and an end of 7 — confirm the table shows 3 through 7.
Try start and end equal to the same number to see what happens.

content.title: Use end + 1 to Include the End Value
content.body: If the user enters 7 as the ending number, range(start, end) would stop at 6. Adding 1 makes it range(start, end + 1), which includes 7 as expected. This is one of the most common off-by-one errors in Python.

A program that asks the user to enter five numbers and calculates their sum using an accumulator variable. The accumulator is initialized to 0.0 before the loop and updated with += inside it. This pattern appears in virtually every Lab assignment.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-17 (6th Ed) or Program 4-12 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named sum_numbers.py.
Type the program exactly, noting where total = 0.0 is placed.
Run it and enter five numbers.
Verify the total is the sum of what you entered.

content.title: Initialize the Accumulator BEFORE the Loop
content.body: If total = 0.0 is inside the loop, it resets to zero on every iteration and you only ever get the last number entered. The accumulator must be set before the loop starts so it can collect values across all iterations.

A retail price calculator with two levels of while loops. The outer loop lets the user process multiple items. The inner loop validates the wholesale cost — if a negative number is entered, the program displays an error and asks again. Only after a valid cost is entered does the retail calculation proceed.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-21 (6th Ed) or Program 4-16 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named retail_with_validation.py.
Type the program carefully, paying attention to both loop levels.
Run it and enter a negative number first — confirm the error message appears.
Enter a valid positive number and confirm the retail price calculates correctly.
Test the outer loop by entering 'y' to process a second item.

content.title: Input Validation Is a Core Pattern
content.body: The inner validation loop is a pattern you will use in almost every Lab assignment with user input: get input, then loop while it's invalid, displaying an error and re-prompting. Get comfortable with this structure now.

A grade calculator that uses nested for loops to process multiple students, each with multiple test scores. The outer loop iterates through each student; the inner loop collects that student's scores and accumulates the total. The average is calculated and printed after the inner loop completes for each student.

items: Open your textbook to Program 4-22 (6th Ed) or Program 4-17 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named test_score_averages.py.
Type the program carefully, paying attention to indentation at each loop level.
Run it with 2 students and 3 test scores each as a first test.
Confirm each student's average is correct and that the accumulator resets between students.

content.title: Reset the Accumulator Inside the Outer Loop
content.body: total = 0.0 must be inside the outer loop (indented under it) but outside the inner loop. If it's before the outer loop, it only resets once. If it's inside the inner loop, it resets after every score. The right place is at the start of each student's processing.

temperature.py runs and output matches the book

finalChecklist.submissionReminder: CodeGrade only keeps your most recent submission. Your final upload must include all 10 files or you will lose credit for any that are missing.
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: These are Chapter 4 BookEx exercises. You are typing Gaddis programs exactly as written. No functions, lists, or techniques from later chapters are needed or expected.

specific: range() with one, two, and three arguments
All Chapter 2–3 techniques: variables, input(), arithmetic, if-else, f-strings
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: functions, lists as data structures, file I/O, anything from chapters not yet covered

ifYouGetStuck: If your program won't stop, press Ctrl+C (Windows) or Command+Period (Mac) to break out of an infinite loop
Re-read the checkpoint and compare your code to the book line by line — a missing update to the loop variable is the most common Chapter 4 bug
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which files pass and which need work
Post in the course Discord if you are stuck after two attempts (describe the error, do not post your code)
Come to Zoom or office hours if you are still stuck
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 4 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdown of every program in this chapter
